title: Cranberry Sauce
subtitle: Sharp, jewel-red sauce for turkey and game
excerpt: Cranberry sauce is one of the easiest things on the Christmas table: cranberries, sugar, and a little orange juice, simmered together for 10 minutes until the berries burst and the liquid sets to a loose, jammy consistency as it cools. The balance between sharp and sweet is the only thing to get right. Make it the day before; it improves overnight in the fridge.
sourceNotes: Cranberry sauce became standard on the British Christmas table in the twentieth century, adopted from American Thanksgiving tradition via post-war cookery writing. Now a fixture alongside turkey. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: redcurrant and berry sauces for game. Modern British method.

content.text: The only judgment call in cranberry sauce is sweetness. Cranberries are very sharp; the amount of sugar needed varies with personal preference and what the sauce is accompanying. Alongside turkey with a sweet chestnut stuffing, a sharper sauce (less sugar) provides contrast. Alongside game, more sugar balances the bitterness. The orange zest is optional but adds a brightness that prevents the sauce from tasting simply sour. Cranberry
; take it off the heat when it still looks loose.

Put {{cranberries}} cranberries, {{caster-sugar}}, the juice and zest of {{orange}}, and {{water}} in a small saucepan. Bring to the boil, stirring to dissolve the sugar. Reduce the heat and simmer for 8 to 10 minutes, stirring occasionally, until most of the berries have burst and the liquid is syrupy. The sauce will look slightly thin; it sets further on cooling. Taste and add more sugar if needed. Pour into a bowl or jar and allow to cool.

rows.fix: Add sugar in stages; taste after each addition. A small amount of redcurrant jelly stirred in at the end balances sharpness without making the sauce sweet.
rows.cause: Frozen cranberries are sometimes sharper than fresh
rows.symptom: Sauce too sharp even with full sugar

rows.fix: Return to the heat and simmer for a further 5 minutes. The sauce should coat the back of a spoon when hot; it will be considerably thicker cold.
rows.cause: Not simmered long enough for the pectin to activate

content.text: Cranberry sauce became a British Christmas standard in the mid-twentieth century. It is now as fixed a part of the Christmas plate as the turkey it accompanies. It is also excellent with cold turkey and ham sandwiches the following day, with a sharp Cheddar, or as a condiment alongside venison and game throughout the autumn.
